original_hypothesis.title: Assertion Confirmation with Stale Challenge Manager via User-Supplied prevConfig
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In confirmAssertion, the winning edge is fetched from IEdgeChallengeManager(prevConfig.challengeManager). The prevConfig is user-supplied but validated against prevAssertion.configHash. If the rollup has been upgraded and a NEW challenge manager deployed, the old challenge manager address is still in the old assertion's configHash. An attacker who won a challenge on the old challenge manager (before upgrade) could use this to confirm assertions after the upgrade, even if the new challenge manager would not allow it. The old challenge manager's state is immutable and the winning edge remains confirmed there.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The configHash mechanism binds each assertion to the challenge manager at the time of creation. After a challenge manager upgrade, old pending assertions still reference the old challenge manager. If the old challenge manager has been exploited or contains different dispute outcomes, these could be used to confirm assertions on the new rollup state.
deployment_assumptions: Challenge manager has been upgraded/changed since the disputed assertion was created
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: deployment.ts shows challengeManager is set as template; prepareBoldUpgrade.ts deploys newEdgeChallengeManager; old manager remains accessible at its address

proof_of_signal.pattern: IEdgeChallengeManager(prevConfig.challengeManager).getEdge(winningEdgeId) uses challenge manager from prevConfig which may reference pre-upgrade contract

preconditions: Old pending assertion still references old challenge manager via configHash
Winning edge exists in old challenge manager
Old challenge manager is still accessible (not self-destructed)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: prevConfig.challengeManager can be ANY address that matches the configHash of prevAssertion
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Use old challenge manager's confirmed edges to validate confirmation of assertions under old rules
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If old challenge manager had different security parameters or was compromised, assertions could be confirmed that wouldn't pass under new manager

economic_questions: Does the upgrade process invalidate old pending assertions?
Can old challenge manager state be relied upon after upgrade?
Is there a mechanism to force re-challenge under new manager?
validation_requirements: Review upgrade procedure for challenge manager transitions
Check if pending assertions are resolved before upgrade
Verify old challenge manager behavior after upgrade

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value dependent on what state transition the confirmed assertion enables
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High if fraudulent state is confirmed
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Depends on when upgrade occurs relative to pending disputes

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During or immediately after challenge manager upgrade

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All rollup users if fraudulent state is confirmed

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Upgrade process, assertion lifecycle during upgrades
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace assertion confirmation path across manager upgrade

validation_result.impact_summary: The code correctly validates prevConfig.challengeManager against prevAssertion.configHash. Using the creation-time challenge manager is by design, not a defect.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that using prevConfig.challengeManager (bound by configHash) to fetch winning edges after a challenge manager upgrade constitutes a vulnerability. However, tracing the code reveals this is intentional design:

1. When an assertion is created in createNewAssertion (RollupCore.sol), the configHash is computed including `address(challengeManager)` at that time.
2. When confirming via confirmAssertion (RollupUserLogic.sol), prevConfig is validated against prevAssertion.configHash, ensuring the challenge manager address matches what was stored at creation time.
3. The winning edge is fetched from that validated challenge manager.

This design correctly ensures disputes are resolved using the rules/challenge manager in effect when the assertions were created. Changing the challenge manager mid-dispute would be incorrect behavior. The scenario described (old CM being exploited after upgrade) is an operational/upgrade procedure concern - administrators should resolve pending disputes before upgrading, or pause the contract during transition. The code itself has no defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The configHash validation at RollupUserLogic.sol line ~97 (RollupLib.validateConfigHash(prevConfig, prevAssertion.configHash)) ensures prevConfig.challengeManager cannot be arbitrary - it must match what was stored when the prev assertion was created. The configHash is computed in RollupCore.sol's createNewAssertion using `address(challengeManager)` at creation time. This is correct: each assertion is bound to its creation-time challenge manager, and disputes should be resolved under those rules.

calling_sequence: 2. RollupLib.validateConfigHash(prevConfig, prevAssertion.configHash) validates prevConfig against stored configHash
3. configHash was set during createNewAssertion with address(challengeManager) at creation time
5. Winning edge checks (claimId, status, confirmedAtBlock, grace period) are performed
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: No vulnerable path exists. The prevConfig.challengeManager is cryptographically bound to the assertion via configHash. The user cannot supply an arbitrary challenge manager - only the one that was active when the prev assertion was created. This binding is by design: disputes should be resolved under the rules in effect at assertion creation time.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Challenge manager has been upgraded/changed since the disputed assertion was created
config_validation.deployment_evidence: prepareBoldUpgrade.ts deploys newEdgeChallengeManager, and deployment.ts shows challengeManager is set as a template via setTemplates. The old challenge manager remains accessible at its address after upgrade. However, the upgrade process is expected to handle pending assertion transitions - this is an operational procedure concern, not a code defect.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires: (1) a challenge manager upgrade occurs, (2) old pending assertions reference old CM via configHash, (3) attacker wins challenge on old CM, (4) attacker confirms assertion using old CM's winning edge. However, this is not a code defect - the configHash mechanism intentionally binds assertions to their creation-time challenge manager. The code correctly validates this binding. If an upgrade needs to invalidate old disputes, this must be handled at the upgrade procedure level (e.g., pausing the contract, resolving pending assertions, or administrative intervention). The contract includes PausableUpgradeable for exactly this purpose.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No code defect exists. The configHash mechanism works correctly as designed. The scenario described is an operational/upgrade procedure concern that should be addressed through proper upgrade governance (pausing, resolving pending disputes, etc.), not through changes to the assertion confirmation logic.
recommended_mitigations: Document upgrade procedures to ensure pending assertions are resolved before challenge manager changes
Use the pause mechanism during challenge manager transitions to prevent confirmations
Consider adding an admin function to force-resolve or invalidate assertions tied to deprecated challenge managers
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy rollup with CM1. 2. Create assertions, initiate dispute on CM1. 3. Upgrade to CM2. 4. Win challenge on CM1. 5. Attempt to confirm assertion using CM1's winning edge. 6. Verify this succeeds (expected - by design). 7. Verify pausing prevents this during upgrade window.
uncertainties: The exact upgrade procedure for challenge manager transitions is not fully specified in the provided code - it depends on external governance/admin logic not in scope
Whether the upgrade process includes automatic resolution of pending assertions is not visible in the provided scripts
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the pause mechanism is sufficient to prevent stale challenge manager confirmations during upgrades
Test the full upgrade flow to ensure pending assertions are properly handled
Verify that admin logic (not in scope) includes mechanisms to handle assertion lifecycle during upgrades
